speaker: interviewer
text: It's February the 5th, 1996. The survivor is Ralph Fischer. Mary Rothschild, interviewer. It's in Los Angeles, California, USA. It will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: It is February 5, 1996. I'm Mary Rothschild interviewing Ralph Fischer for the Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. It's February 5, 1996. Tape one with Ralph Fischer. Please give us your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Ralph Fischer. I was born in Lodz, Poland on November 28, 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my father's name was Betsalel. My mother's name was Rosa, Rosa Verger.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good, loving parents. My father was strict, but was just. What can-- I-- I didn't live with them too long enough to appreciate them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Conservative, let's put it that way. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they celebrate the Jewish holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the Jewish holiday was celebrated. Friday evening, we close the shop. Kosher. But I would say my father didn't daven every morning, and we didn't either. But holidays observe, every holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbat wasn't always Shabbat. No shul, just [NON-ENGLISH], you know, and [NON-ENGLISH]. It was good food-- traditional.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were four all together. I had a brother and two sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name was Harry. I had a older sister-- he was the oldest. There was an older sister for me, and it was Leah. And the little one, her name was Mary, or Miriam we called her.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of house did you grow up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: A good, loving house. We were upper-- upper middle class, I would say. Because I-- I-- I went to private school. And my brother, he wanted to be a dentist. He couldn't get place in a Polish university because of the numerous clauses in our youth. And my father did his best. And we sent him to Italy. And in Milan he became a dentist, and he came back. So financially, we were OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish and Yiddish. The kids, between them, Polish. And between the parents, Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't. They-- when I-- when I came to the world, there was no grandparents. I knew they died. My mother was from a little city, Zawiercie. My father was from my little city, Dobra. And I never had the joy to play with my grandfather, grandmother, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I've got a lot of cousins, because my father has four sisters in Poland. They all had a bunch of kids. So we were, say, a family of 40 maybe-- cousins and nephews and nieces, you know. It was a large family. We seen each other very often. All the same house, everything was together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any favorites?

speaker: interviewee
text: Between whom?

speaker: interviewer
text: Between your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was an uncle of mine, my mother's brother. Which was a smart guy, usually had conversation with me. Anybody pushed me away, he used to explain to me things. Very knowledgeable man. He died in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Moishe Faivel, born in Zawiercie. Yes, he was my favorite. He was a good uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a private school, the elementary. Then it was [NON-ENGLISH] on the [NON-ENGLISH] street in Lodz. And then I have two years of the [NON-ENGLISH] gymnasium in Lodz. And then came 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about school?

speaker: interviewee
text: School? I like school, you know. We had homework to do, which we didn't like. We played hard. We get to know our things. We had to-- we have to learn. It was all-- it was all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any subjects you liked?

speaker: interviewee
text: History and geography I liked very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it an exclusively Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, as a matter of fact, the whole neighborhood was exclusive Jewish. The only Christians, Poles, were the janitor or the [NON-ENGLISH], we used to call them. Or the policemen in the street. Just everything was Jewish. As a matter of fact, we didn't go Saturday to school. We used to go Sunday to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Oh, yeah-- the-- the janitor's son, Sigmund. And he played with us, of course. He spoke Yiddish like I did. That was the only [NON-ENGLISH]. And then, later on, I belonged to a club, [NON-ENGLISH]. And soccer we played. And we played soccer, we usually played against the Poles. But I wouldn't say friend. Close friends, no. Only Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: We manufactured leather coats, either atelier or a workshop, in the apartment. It was a very big apartment, so two rooms were the atelier. Seven people worked. We manufactured and sold to stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: About whom?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a strict man, a just man. He was-- he was quiet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's all. And we-- we respected him. The respect for parents was very grand. I used to kiss-- when I went to school, I used to kiss their hands. When I come back from school, I was supposed to kiss the hands. That was the way. You couldn't touch my father's chair at the table. That was taboo. That's what's still in my memory, vivid, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a very intelligent woman. She was perfect in Russian and German and French. A Yiddish mother, busy with the kids, and busy with the kitchen. And if I needed a question for school, I didn't go to father. I go to mother. She was much younger than my father. She was 15 years younger than my father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oh, my father was a very handsome man. So no wonder she married him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a big city, a lot of culture. And we didn't have television or computers or internet. We read-- we read a lot of books. Books and you would had conversation of books. And we went to the movies from time to time, if we had the 15 grosze. But it was-- it was a city-- with a philharmonic, with a orchestra, 60 some movie theaters, two Yiddish theaters, and about four Polish theaters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of books did you read?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kinds. All kinds, from Polish, [NON-ENGLISH], Moliere, Shakespeare, Proust, Dostoevsky. No, we read good books. Sometimes trash, too. But that was-- but mostly, I remember was good books.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a hobby?

speaker: interviewee
text: A kid, a hobby? No, I was too busy for hobbies. I liked to ride my bicycle. I liked to play soccer. This was a hobby. And girls, of course, later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your fondest memories of your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. There was many of them. The holidays, I think, when we all came together. And my friends, we used to go out to the park, and have conversations. There's a lot of memories, but they're fading away. They're fading away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any incidents of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. One of my cousins got stabbed in the streets by a Pole. And there was times when we wandered into the Polish section-- they used to call us dirty Jew, or chased us with stones to go away. Yes, I-- I didn't have big love for the Poles-- because of that. Because I felt Polish. I spoke their language perfectly. I-- I was absorbed with their culture, and I was still a dirty Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my memories about that are very negative. Any particular incident sticks in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: About anti-Semitism? Oh, there was a pogrom in [NON-ENGLISH] if you remember-- no, you don't remember. There was a big pogrom in [NON-ENGLISH]. And after there was a court. And I followed it. And it was so unjust-- it reminded me of OJ Simpson's court.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? Oh, the Poles who were arrested got free. Two Jews got condemned, because they defend themselves. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I tell you? You're talking about my childhood, huh? And those kind of incidents? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Actually, nothing comes to my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the first day of war?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first day of war--it was September 1, 1940-- 1939. My mother woke me up. She says, there's war. And then we put on the radio, and we heard about the war. Four days later, the-- then there was the few planes flew over Lodz. They didn't throw bombs at Lodz, because Lodz was a very German city. 30% were Germans. So there was no damage of a bombing-- bombardment. And then the Germans start approaching. And on the radio it said, all young men have to leave town and run towards Warsaw. Me and my brother and another, about four guys from the same building, from the same house, we start running to-- towards Warsaw. On the-- on the way we were strifed by German planes. So we hid in the woods. At night we marched. For three, four days we came to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my brother, Harry. There was two brothers, Olshev, who lived there, and my brother's friend. His name was-- what was his name? Moeshe Moniac-- Moniac. And we came to Warsaw. And they told us to dig ditches. And then I thought they gave us rifles without bullets. So we left our rifles and went to town. And I had a place to sleep in a bakery. He let me in, as long as I clean up. They do a lot of matzah over there, so I lived on matzah for three, four days. Anyway Warsaw fell, after two or three weeks, I think, of-- of bitter fightings and bombardments. We went back to Lodz, also walking to Lodz. I remember, I came back home-- all my toenails became black and fall off, from all 10 toes. Because I didn't take off my shoes for weeks. But then they grew back, like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after you came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: After we came back, it starts with restrictions. There was a curfew. You couldn't go out from the house at 5 o'clock. Of course, we live in the-- in the building-- we leave, after 5 o'clock, we used to play cards. Well, the adults used to play cards. We used to just watch them, that's all. And, of course, politics. We talked politics. At that time, four or five days they-- the war would be over. But it didn't happen. And then came the bad times of ghetto. The Germans start to build-- they built, prepare to pull all the Jews from town to the ghetto. It took them a few months. And finally, they made the ghetto. To make the ghetto, they took away a piece of Lodz, which was like a suburb. It was a-- the apartments were-- the people who lived there was Poles, but all government functionaries, or big in the factories. And it looked like-- like America-- small houses with little back yards. My father had a friend from his town, Dobra. And he used to buy coats from us for-- for this family. And he came to us as said here, I have to leave my house. Here are the keys. And these are permits from the Germans. Take over. I know it's going to be in good hands. We moved over there. It was charming-- three rooms, a little garden, a goat, a lot of fruits, and other vegetation, a lot of salad. I was working. It was beautiful. And at that time, food was no problem. There was enough bread, and there was enough flour. And it was-- the paradise was about six, seven months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1940. 1940 in the fall. One day, I-- my father-- my father was instructor in a resort. And I used to go to hashara and walk on the land. There was hasharas in the ghetto. And ours was from our gymnasium-- the used the [NON-ENGLISH]. And that was home-- during some days I was often was home. And I was in the garden once, and in front of our house stopped-- you know, the head of the Lodz ghetto was a crazy man, Mordechai Rumkowski. He was an older man, entoured with young girls, with body guards. And he came and stopped his coach, with-- white horse, with two bodyguards-- and descended, opened the door. Looked around, it was beautiful, because I took care of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, who lives here? And I say, well, my father is Fischer. He-- he-- he works in a resort. And he left. A day later, an office of the police came. Says, you've got three days to move out of that house. I says, why? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I ran and told my father. And my father starts running around. And nothing-- it was Rumkowski's order. A captain of the Jewish police was supposed to live in that house. I was mad. And a day before we had to move, they gave us one room on the Jerusalimska, a very crummy section. And I helped all the-- all the-- all the pears, all the apples that weren't even ripe, all the salads I helped out from doing it, and took with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't think nothing of it, but about a week later, at night, the police came-- the Jewish police came, and arrested me. And why? They didn't tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my father found out, because I-- I took all those vegetables out. I destroyed the garden. The kept me in the Czarnieckiego-- it was a Jewish prison-- for a few nights. They let my family bring me a lot of clothing, a bag full of clothing. A few days later at night, they took us out, about 20.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who were the 20? Most were criminals, Jewish criminals, butchers-- all the rough necks. I was the only little guy over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us out of the ghetto to a prison, a Polish prison, [NON-ENGLISH], it was a Polish prison. At that prison, they kept us for a few weeks until they accumulate Jews from around the neighborhoods-- [NON-ENGLISH], all around Lodz, about 1,000. We were young men-- the oldest in the 40s, that's all. And put us in a cattle train, and we traveled to a city called Poznan-- Posen. It was before the war, it was most German. And as a matter of fact, became the-- the Third Reich. And over there, not in the city, in the [NON-ENGLISH], before the city, they took it off. You went to a encampment. There was a-- there was a gate. When they opened the gate, that was the first shock of my life. [CRYING] Sorry. They opened the gate and there was a gallows-- six ropes with the-- a hangman's noose. And there was a sign on top of it, [GERMAN]. Which in translation would be, Jew, think about it. You are the calamity-- you are the-- you are the-- the destruction of all of the people. That's it. That was-- that was my welcome. All right, OK, it was a primitive camp, because over there, they were the stables of the Polish cavalry. The barracks was taken already by Germans and Poles. And we took over the stables. For a while, we slept just on the floor. Work was very hard. At 4 o'clock, they wake us up. We went to the [NON-ENGLISH], which they built over there a huge network of railroads. About 100 railroads together, in a-- in a-- there's supposed to be a changing place. You see, trains came from all over the country, they-- they take off of their wagons and move them to-- to special rails, and-- and put them on the special trains. And they went all over-- all over Europe. And we were to build this. There was-- we-- Polish Jews, there were a lot of Poles, but not prisoners. They were workers, and a lot of Germans, and British prisoners of war, and the hondons-- the first time I saw British. They were all captured in Dunkirk. Anyway, I was lucky. They chose me to be a [NON-ENGLISH]. I used to move-- you see, to-- they did a lot of grading from ground to take off hills. You had-- you had little trains with a lot a little wagons, and they used to fill with dirt, and move from place to place. And I used to move the trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's see, I think oh, maybe we have to go left, I turn the [NON-ENGLISH]. Right, turn the [NON-ENGLISH]. So I was fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to remember which train is there, because if a train was running down the hill, I was suppose to stop the next train, and move them on a waiting line. If I made a mistake, it would kill me. But I-- I managed this. And the work wasn't hard. Sometimes I had to shovel some coal through the-- through the wagons, because they weren't very good. And I-- and the British used to pass by and throw me cigarettes. One day a German, an officer had come over. Hey, show me what you've  got. And I had a pack of English cigarettes. And he took it away from me. But some I used to take to camp, and exchange for more bread, or whatever. But actually, there was no hunger.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, the night shift was a pall. And he used to bring me some food. And then one day he asked me, do you have tailors in your shop over there? I said sure, we've got shoe makers and tailors. He says, would you-- could you fix my shoes? I said, yeah, bring it over. And he bring me shoes. I hided them, and gave it to-- to the shoemakers. I want my money first. What do I get? You're going to get what I going to get.

speaker: interviewee
text: And took it out, and fixed it. He used to give me two, three breads. And I used to split with him. So there was no problem. I even give food to my friends. It was good, only for a while. Then came a company. Their name is [GERMAN]. They still exist in Germany, very big. And they picked out 20-- it was-- it was a-- a-- a Sunday morning-- they picked out 20 from us. And for the work. It was hard work. A lot of beating, because the foreman, were German- Germans. But in Poland, they used to call them Volksdeutsche. There was a lot of beating around. I was lucky. I was again lucky. That's why I survived, maybe. My-- my Obermeister was a Fischer. When he took our names, so, you know, one of us [NON-ENGLISH], one was [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]. I said Fischer. He says, 'One of us has a human name.' And he took me to help him carry the tree pots for the-- he was a surveyor. So it was good too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were they down? Yeah, in that-- in that time, if they finished, let's say, about a mile of work, railroad, used to take us with them. We didn't go to the-- to the-- back to the camp, but they made a little shed. And we slept over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: The work was very hard, very hard. We used to work 14 hours, and then we went to sleep. And the train came in at night, they used to wake us up, of course, with beating. And you had to unload the trains. And if it took til 5 o'clock in the morning, we never get to go back to the-- to the camp, to the-- to the barracks. We used to work done. So sometimes 24 hours. That was Gutenbrunn. I am surprised I don't see the name of that camp, because thousands of young Jewish lives were wasted over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near the city of Poznan, around. In Polish, they call it [POLISH]. And there was a-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Poland. And that moment, they took us-- it became Germany. They took it with the Reich. But it was Poland, Poznan of Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1941. '41 till '42, I think. And we traveled with that [GERMAN]. And finally, after finish that stretch of work, they send us back to that camp. And that camp, when I came back, I see there's not too many people. They made selections. I didn't know about it. They used to call this at that time-- that was before the selections, they called it [NON-ENGLISH]. They went on the [NON-ENGLISH]. Later on, I knew that they went to Treblinka. Anyway, I was picked with 40, and they put us on trains. And we went to another camp. It's irony-- four kilometers from Lodz. They call it Adrzejow, also a Jewish camp. Yeah, a matter of fact, we only Jews, Polish Jews, only Jews. It was a Jewish camp. And we cleared-- there was a forest. We had to cut the trees. And the head of the camp was also a Jew. Until-- there was-- there was a woman camp too. Just close, a woman camp. I-- I met my cousin over here, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we worked-- was it '42? Til [? end of ?] '42, I think. It was winter, I remember, when they liquidated that camp. Because we couldn't work in the snow. And I was sent to Birkenau, Auschwitz. Winter of what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you arrive in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: By-- by cattle train, of course. I didn't went to the [? train ?], like people tell me, there was no women or no children. It was only us. And that took us off from the train. But, another thing, the guys-- which was the commander-- took us on the train, recognized a few guys from Posen, and they recognize me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one was over there and says, hey, Ralph, you know who is here? And he gave me the name of Husley. Husley was a friend of mine. We slept in the same-- he slept above me in Gutenbrunn. He says he's-- he's a [NON-ENGLISH] in the bakery. Just tell him about me, tell him. Well they took us to the barrack-- over there was a selection, you know? I remember that guy in the white uniform. Murderer-- left, right. Anyway, we went into camp-- Which guy was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was '42. This was--what was his name? Mengele. Mengele. Mengele, the famous Mengele. And we went to the barracks. And the work, we did the work. They chased us out. Appells every time and counting-- counting like we were treasures or something. And that Husley showed up. Ah, we kissed. He says, well, listen, tomorrow I'll take you to my Oberscharfuhrer over the bakery. And you will tell him that you are baker-- your father was a baker, your grandfather was a baker. I said, all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took me to him. And he said, good, yeah, [GERMAN]. And I worked in the bakery.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I wasn't hungry. I had bread, which I used to steal a few and go to the camp and give to my friends. And one day-- I didn't cry in camp. I didn't have any tears. I didn't notice it. I was mad. I was angry- anguish. And one day, it was in the morning, I was pushing a cart, with loaves of bread. And I see a column coming towards me. This was out of the camp. And there were children. [CRYING] Little children-- 10 years old and beyond. The big children hold little children in their arms, and by hand. One girl from-- by now, I couldn't take off the eyes-- she was about 10. She held a girl in her arm, and the girl had a doll as big as she was. And I knew where they're going. I knew they were going to the gas chambers. Then I cried. I still remember the salt in my mouth. I didn't want to sob, because the Germans were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what was so stupid-- 30 children, and two SS men with machine guns, escorting them from both sides. I says, what kind of people is that? Who are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what I think-- this is what I think was the biggest shock of my life. I couldn't sleep for nights. OK, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Auschwitz was finished. This was already '43. I didn't get a tattoo, because I-- I didn't suppose to be in Auschwitz. I was a [NON-ENGLISH]-- in transit. I was still not named about 11 months. And they put us on trains. And we traveled quite a few days. And we came to a camp called Mauthausen in Austria, up in the Alps.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. It was-- it was OK. We didn't have any-- any-- any wagon-lits, you know, but it was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of clothes did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I had the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, the striped, like the zebra has.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shoes? There were shoes. Usually there was shoes with wooden soles. But you can have your own shoes. I bought some shoes in Auschwitz for bread. So I had good shoes. And we came to Mauthausen. That was a long-established camp. Like the sardines-- they push-- this was early in our-- I think Russia was attacking the Germans, and pushing down. And that-- that felt really uncomfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a lot of people coming from-- from some camps. We slept like the sardines. And the morning-- We have to stop now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, fine.
